questions.question: Derive the relation between speed, frequency, and wavelength of sound.
questions.answer: Speed of sound v = frequency × wavelength, v = f × λ. It comes from the fact that in one period T, the wave travels one wavelength: v = λ / T and f = 1/T, so v = f × λ.

questions.question: Explain why sound waves are longitudinal in nature.
questions.answer: Sound waves involve particle vibrations along the direction of wave propagation, causing compressions and rarefactions; thus, they are longitudinal.

questions.question: Derive the expression for the frequency of a vibrating string fixed at both ends.
questions.answer: Fundamental frequency f = v / 2L, where v is speed of wave on string and L is length. The string vibrates in standing wave pattern; wavelength λ = 2L for fundamental, so f = v / λ = v / 2L.

questions.question: Explain how resonance occurs and give an example.
questions.answer: Resonance occurs when a system is forced to vibrate at its natural frequency, leading to large amplitude. Example: tuning fork making another identical fork vibrate.

questions.question: Explain the effect of temperature on the speed of sound in air.
questions.answer: Speed of sound increases with temperature because higher temperature increases the kinetic energy of air molecules, allowing faster vibration transfer. v ≈ 331 + 0.6T m/s.

questions.question: Explain why the pitch of sound changes when a source approaches or recedes from the listener (Doppler effect).
questions.answer: When source approaches, waves are compressed → higher frequency → higher pitch. When source recedes, waves are stretched → lower frequency → lower pitch.

questions.question: Derive the expression for the time period of an echo heard from a distant wall.
questions.answer: Time period t = 2d / v, where d is distance to wall and v is speed of sound. The sound travels to the wall and back, so total distance = 2d.

questions.question: Explain the formation of beats when two sound waves of slightly different frequencies interfere.
questions.answer: Beats occur due to constructive and destructive interference of two waves with close frequencies. Beat frequency f_beat = |f1 - f2|.

questions.question: Explain why the intensity of sound decreases with distance from the source.
questions.answer: Sound spreads over a larger area as it propagates, reducing energy per unit area. Intensity I ∝ 1/r², where r is distance from source.

questions.question: Explain how ultrasound is used in SONAR to detect underwater objects.
questions.answer: Ultrasound waves are transmitted underwater. They reflect from objects and return. Time of flight t and speed v are used: distance = v × t / 2.

questions.question: Derive the expression for the fundamental frequency of air column in a closed pipe.
questions.answer: Fundamental frequency f = v / 4L, where L is length of pipe and v is speed of sound. Closed pipe has λ = 4L for first harmonic.

questions.question: Derive the expression for the fundamental frequency of air column in an open pipe.
questions.answer: Fundamental frequency f = v / 2L, where L is length of pipe. Open pipe supports λ = 2L for first harmonic.

questions.question: Explain how bats use ultrasound for navigation.
questions.answer: Bats emit ultrasonic waves; waves reflect from obstacles, and bats detect the echoes to determine distance and direction.

questions.question: Explain why low-pitched sounds are better for long-distance communication.
questions.answer: Low-frequency sounds are absorbed less by the medium and scatter less, allowing them to travel longer distances.

questions.question: Explain why sound travels faster in warm air than in cold air.
questions.answer: Warm air molecules move faster, transmitting vibrations quicker. Speed v ≈ 331 + 0.6T m/s.

questions.question: Explain why we cannot hear ultrasonic waves but bats can.
questions.answer: Ultrasound frequency > 20 kHz, beyond human hearing range. Bats have specialized ears to detect these frequencies.

questions.question: Explain how multiple reflections of sound produce reverberation and how it differs from echo.
questions.answer: Reverberation: multiple rapid reflections prolong sound. Echo: a single reflection delayed > 0.1 s. Reverberation is continuous, echo is distinct.

questions.question: Explain why musical instruments produce sound of definite pitch.
questions.answer: Musical instruments produce periodic vibrations with specific frequencies, giving definite pitch.

questions.question: Explain why sound cannot travel through vacuum using particle theory.
questions.answer: Sound requires particle vibration to propagate. In vacuum, absence of particles prevents transmission of vibrations, so sound cannot travel.

questions.question: Explain the principle of echo-sounding for measuring sea depth.
questions.answer: Ultrasound pulse is sent from ship; time t for echo to return is measured. Depth d = v × t / 2, where v is speed of sound in water.

questions.question: Explain why sound waves in air are longitudinal, but light waves are transverse.
questions.answer: Sound needs particle vibration along propagation (longitudinal), while light is electromagnetic and oscillates perpendicular to propagation (transverse).

questions.question: Explain how sonar helps submarines detect underwater obstacles.
questions.answer: Submarine emits ultrasonic waves; waves reflect from obstacles. Time of return and speed of sound allow calculation of distance and position of obstacles.

questions.question: Explain how intensity of sound relates to amplitude.
questions.answer: Intensity ∝ (Amplitude)². Larger amplitude vibrations transmit more energy per unit area, producing louder sound.

questions.question: Explain the difference between infrasonic and ultrasonic waves in terms of frequency and applications.
questions.answer: Infrasonic: <20 Hz, used for earthquake detection; Ultrasonic: >20 kHz, used in medical imaging and sonar.

questions.question: Explain why Doppler effect is used in speed detection of vehicles.
questions.answer: Moving vehicle changes frequency of reflected sound waves; measuring frequency shift allows calculation of speed using Doppler formula.
